text: The preoperative CAT scan displays a bilobed supraumbilical hernia with a bridge between the lobes. The sagittal view reveals the hernia's extent along the longitudinal axis, with a flimsy peritoneum visible above.
The surgical field shows three left-sided ports placed for the procedure. The area is relatively free of adhesions, contrary to expectations based on the preoperative CAT scan.
The surgeon removes the omentum, revealing ethobond sutures from the previous hybrid repair, where a small incision was made over the hernia defect and closed.
So we're still trying to figure out exactly how this hernia occurred. And we'll see a couple of things during the repair that likely led for this to happen.

With the adhesions removed, the surgeon examines the surgical field, identifying the superior hernia defect located above the peritoneal fold and the falciform ligament. The decision is made to preserve the peritoneum as a posterior layer.

The surgeon begins retrorectus dissection at the inferior edge of the progrip mesh from the previous operation. Attempts to remove the mesh reveal it is adhered to the muscle, prompting the surgeon to detach the posterior sheath from the mesh instead. The dissection progresses inferiorly due to the ease of access in that direction.

The surgeon dissects at the level of the umbilicus, addressing a small umbilical defect. The dissection proceeds along the medial edge of the right rectus muscle, revealing contributory perforator vessels from the epigastric arteries. The surgeon carefully preserves the epigastric vessels while dissecting laterally to separate the posterior rectus sheath from the rectus muscle.

The surgeon returns to the challenging area after completing the inferior dissection, attempting to separate the posterior sheath from the pro-grip mesh. A line is marked towards the superior lateral edge of the defect, aiming to dissect laterally beyond the mesh.

The surgeon continues to dissect the posterior sheath from the hernia ring, with the mesh visible above. A neurovascular bundle is identified during the dissection. The cut transversus muscle is observed as the procedure progresses in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon observes that the muscle is thicker and extends further medially. Due to the wide lateral defect from the previous dissection, the surgeon inadvertently begins an upper transverse abdominis release (TAR). To improve visibility, the surgeon attempts to take down the peritoneum.
The surgeon attempts to join the tissue planes by manipulating the peritoneum above, aiming to leave it as a posterior layer while continuing the dissection downward.

And it just, it seemed very confusing to me. I'm looking up above again, trying to see where the edge of the defect is. And I'm realizing with this peritoneum, I just can't see the lateral edge of the defect. So I kind of opened it up to kind of see. And now I'm thinking, well, wait a minute. I opened the posterior sheath so far further lateral because everything had blown open. And it was just really confusing to me. So at that point, I decided to just go

In the right lower quadrant below the arcuate line, the surgeon dissects the superficial transversalis fascia to access the TAR plane, stripping down the peritoneum. Small holes are made in the fascia, which are later closed. The surgeon performs lateral dissection, sweeping the peritoneum down while maintaining a bloodless field, and continues the TAR dissection towards the upper abdomen.

The surgeon works laterally to transition the dissection medially, focusing on taking down the transverse allofascia and pre-transverse allofascia to expose the bare muscle of the transversus abdominis.

The surgeon navigates to the challenging area, identifying nerve bundles and ensuring to stay medial to them. The transversus abdominis muscle is hooked, revealing that an upper TAR (Transversus Abdominis Release) is being performed, and the surgeon proceeds to complete this dissection with increased confidence.

The surgeon re-enters the pre-transversalis plane, carefully dissecting to remove the peritoneum and attenuated hernia sac, revealing the edge of the hernia defect. The dissection progresses smoothly in the unviolated space, facilitating the continuation of the transversus abdominis release (TAR).

And what I think I'm realizing with this, because of where the lateral edge of the defect is, and I never really identified the rectus or muscle, I think what probably happened in this case is that the dissection and crossing over the midline above the hernia in the epigastric region actually went above the linea alba and was in the subcutaneous space. And the surgeon then came back down into the proper retro-rectus space on the other side, but because they closed the defect through an open hybrid two centimeter incision over only the supraumbilical defect, they never closed the defect that they inadvertently created in the upper abdomen.
The surgeon performs a smooth lateral dissection along the costal margin, ensuring adequate posterior coverage while maintaining a clear surgical field.

The surgeon approaches the xiphoid process, revealing diaphragmatic fibers. The xiphoid is visible superior to the right arm instrument. The completed dissection shows the cut edge of the transversus abdominis, along with the superior and inferior hernia defects. The surgeon prepares to begin the left-sided dissection by placing right-sided ports.
